stories.headline: Colorado's top Medicaid official resigns amid bipartisan legislative pressure
stories.summary:
Kim Bimestefer, the eight-year director of the Colorado Department of Health Care Policy and Financing, announced her resignation Monday, effective April 10. Her departure came as 28 of the state Senate's 35 members had signed onto a resolution authored by Sen. Kyle Mullica, D-Thornton, calling on Gov. Jared Polis to remove her from office.

The resolution cited years of alleged mismanagement, including tens of millions of dollars in Medicaid transportation billing overpayments that went undetected during an internal review, roughly $75 million in potential overpayments for autism care under federal scrutiny, and the loss of Medicaid coverage for more than 500,000 Coloradans during the post-pandemic eligibility unwind.

Bimestefer's agency, known as HCPF, carries a total annual budget of $18 billion, with $4.2 billion from the state's general fund, and has surpassed K-12 education as the largest line item in Colorado's budget. Polis accepted her resignation, praising her commitment while acknowledging the difficult circumstances facing the department.

Mullica called the leadership change a necessary step toward accountability and said it creates an opportunity to stabilize the department and place the Medicaid program on a sustainable long-term path, particularly as lawmakers grapple with a $1.5 billion statewide budget shortfall.

stories.headline: Polis signs law requiring social media companies to comply with police warrants within 72 hours
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis signed Senate Bill 26-011 on Monday, requiring social media platforms to acknowledge law enforcement search warrants within eight hours and fully comply within 72 hours. Companies must also maintain a staffed 24/7 hotline for law enforcement. The bipartisan measure was sponsored by a Republican-Democratic team in both the House and Senate.

Law enforcement officials say warrants sent to social media companies are increasingly being ignored or significantly delayed, hampering criminal investigations ranging from stolen property cases to drug trafficking. Supporters at the signing ceremony included families who have pushed for faster tech-platform cooperation in criminal cases.

Speaker Pro Tempore Andy Boesenecker argued the law simply holds social media companies to the same legal standard as any other party served with a warrant. Former deputy district attorney and bill co-sponsor Sen. Dylan Roberts said delays in accessing digital evidence carry real consequences for victims and the pursuit of justice.

A separate measure, House Bill 26-1255, sponsored by Rep. Tammy Story and born from the Evergreen High School shooting, would go further by requiring social media companies to proactively report to law enforcement when taking action against a user for certain conduct.

stories.headline: Colorado legislature enters final stretch with budget, AI regulation and housing bills on the floor
stories.summary:
The Colorado Joint Budget Committee is expected to finalize the state's budget this week, with the document set to be introduced in the House next week. The budget's $1.5 billion shortfall has dominated the legislative session since January and will trigger a week of floor debates and amendment votes once introduced.

Ahead of the budget's formal introduction, both chambers are moving quickly to clear other legislation. The House is scheduled to debate bills regulating AI use in psychotherapy, modifying eligibility rules for Proposition 123 housing funds, and shielding minors' name-change records from public view.

The Senate this week passed bills regulating 3D-printed firearms and renaming César Chávez Day for farmworkers. Additional Senate debates are scheduled on limiting credit card swipe fees, opposing conservative-backed fiscal ballot measures, and maternal health legislation.

With roughly six weeks remaining in the 2026 session, the pace of bill signings by Gov. Polis is also accelerating as the legislature moves toward adjournment.

stories.headline: Rockies snap 0-3 skid with 14-5 rout of Toronto Blue Jays
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies earned their first win of the 2026 season Monday night, defeating the Toronto Blue Jays 14-5 at Rogers Centre. Troy Johnston hit a two-run home run and Ezequiel Tovar recorded three hits and three RBIs to lead the offensive outburst.

The victory ended a frustrating start to the season in which the Rockies dropped three consecutive one-run games, including a series sweep in Miami. The 14-run performance marked a sharp reversal for an offense that had struggled to capitalize on scoring opportunities in the season's first three games.

Right-hander Tomoyuki Sugano made the start for Colorado. The win gives the Rockies their first entry in the win column as the club heads toward its home opener at Coors Field later this week.

stories.headline: Avalanche rout Flames 9-2, Broncos GM says Bo Nix ahead of schedule in ankle recovery
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche defeated the Calgary Flames 9-2 on Tuesday, March 31, continuing their strong play atop the NHL standings. Goaltender Scott Wedgewood spoke to media following the blowout victory. The result follows the Avalanche's recent home-and-home series with Winnipeg and extends their dominant stretch of play.

In separate news, Broncos general manager George Paton said Monday at the NFL annual meetings in Phoenix that quarterback Bo Nix is ahead of schedule in his recovery from ankle surgery performed in late January. Paton told 9NEWS that Nix is already running and jumping and is expected to be fully ready for organized team activities beginning in late May.

Nix suffered a clean ankle fracture during the AFC Championship Game, a 10-7 loss to New England that ended Denver's season at 15-3. Paton expressed confidence that Nix will be full-go for the start of the offseason program in early May, calling his rehab effort exemplary.

stories.headline: Avalanche sign undrafted forward Matt DiMarsico to two-year entry-level contract
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche announced Monday they have signed forward Matt DiMarsico to a two-year, entry-level contract beginning with the 2026-27 season. DiMarsico will join the Avalanche's AHL affiliate, the Colorado Eagles, on an amateur tryout for the remainder of this season.

DiMarsico, a winger from Wexford, Pa., went undrafted after three seasons at Penn State University, where he posted 42 goals and 58 assists for 100 points in 111 games. This season he scored 18 goals and added 24 assists for 42 points in 37 games, leading the Nittany Lions in goals.

Avalanche scouts project DiMarsico as a potential two-way player at the professional level, citing his scoring ability, playmaking and defensive-zone work in college. Penn State was eliminated in the first round of this year's NCAA tournament by Minnesota Duluth.

stories.headline: Denver-area insurance broker indicted on 14 felony counts for nearly $100,000 in fraud
stories.summary:
A Colorado statewide grand jury indicted George Gonzalez, 55, owner of Amerimex Insurance, on nine counts of insurance fraud and five counts of theft, all felonies, the Colorado Attorney General's Office announced Monday. Gonzalez is accused of diverting approximately $97,233 in customer insurance payments to himself rather than remitting the funds to contracted insurance companies.

According to the indictment, Gonzalez brokered workers' compensation insurance policies through Pinnacol Assurance. When customers paid Amerimex, he allegedly reported lower payment amounts to Pinnacol and kept the difference. The scheme was uncovered when a new customer called Pinnacol directly to verify his policy and discovered a discrepancy.

Pinnacol investigators identified eight fraudulent policies created between February 2022 and January 2024 and reported Gonzalez to the Colorado Department of Law's Financial Fraud Office. State investigators subsequently identified eight additional companies that had also been victimized.

Attorney General Phil Weiser called insurance fraud a serious crime that ultimately affects all consumers. Individuals who made payments to Amerimex Insurance between 2023 and 2026 and believe they may have been overcharged are asked to contact the AG's office at amerimexinfo@coag.gov.

stories.headline: Broomfield police investigate repeated drugging of pets with meth-laced hot dogs near high school
stories.summary:
Broomfield police are investigating a series of incidents in which someone allegedly placed hot dogs laced with methamphetamine and MDMA in the backyard of a home near Broomfield High School, causing multiple dogs to become ill. The incidents began in late November at a residence on West 6th Avenue near Agate Street, about a block south of the school.

In the first incident, a homeowner discovered two hot dogs containing a crystal substance that later tested positive for methamphetamine. Roughly a month later, two dogs from the property showed unusual behavior after ingesting hot dogs and tested positive for meth. The most recent incident occurred last week, with one dog testing positive for both methamphetamine and MDMA.

Detectives have reviewed surveillance footage, conducted neighborhood canvasses and used a K-9 unit to attempt to trace the origin of the drugged food, but no viable suspects have been identified. Police say the pattern of incidents suggests the specific residence is being intentionally targeted.

Anyone with information is asked to contact Detective Zachary Yee at zyee@broomfield.org or the Broomfield Police non-emergency line at 303-438-6400.

stories.headline: CBI issues missing person alert for Thornton teenager who may be in mental health crisis
stories.summary:
The Colorado Bureau of Investigation is asking for the public's help locating 17-year-old Sladen Capps, who was last seen on March 20 in the 9200 block of York Street in Thornton. Capps has been missing for more than 10 days.

Capps is approximately six feet tall and 150 pounds, with blond hair and blue eyes. He was last seen wearing jeans, an Air Force One hooded sweatshirt and black shoes. Law enforcement believes he may be experiencing a mental health crisis that could place him in danger.

Anyone with information about Capps' whereabouts is asked to call 911 or the Adams County Sheriff's Office at 303-288-1535.

stories.headline: Record-warm winter fuels pine beetle outbreak along Colorado Front Range, raising wildfire risk
stories.summary:
Colorado's warmest winter on record has created conditions for a significant mountain pine and spruce beetle outbreak along the Front Range, according to the Colorado State Forest Service. Aerial surveys conducted last September showed beetle activity surging in pockets across the region, with state forester Matthew McCombs warning of a potentially record-breaking proliferation.

Cold winter temperatures historically keep beetle populations in check by killing larvae, but this year's mild conditions eliminated that natural control. Simultaneously, the severe drought has weakened trees' ability to produce sap, which normally traps beetles and expels them from entry holes, leaving forests largely defenseless.

Beetle-killed trees dry out rapidly and become prime wildfire fuel, a dynamic that has contributed to some of Colorado's most destructive fires. The situation is particularly concerning along the Front Range, where dense communities, homes and infrastructure sit adjacent to at-risk forested land.

Gov. Polis announced the creation of the Ponderosa Mountain Pine Beetle Task Force in December to coordinate a cross-jurisdictional response. Forecasters warn that a warm, dry spring — currently the prevailing prediction — could accelerate the outbreak unless significant precipitation arrives in April.

stories.headline: Storm to bring mountain snow and cooler temperatures to Denver metro Tuesday, with another system possible this weekend
stories.summary:
A potent storm system is expected to bring slushy snow to Colorado's mountains and cooler, wetter conditions to the Denver metro area Tuesday, following days of near-record heat. Winter Weather Advisories are in effect for mountain and foothills areas, with two to 10 inches of slushy snow forecast. Scattered rain showers and highs only in the upper 50s to low 60s are expected along the Front Range.

Rain chances and cooler air are expected to linger into Wednesday, with the best chance for more widespread showers arriving Wednesday evening before skies clear overnight. Temperatures are forecast to rebound to the low 70s Thursday before another system arrives heading into the weekend.

The weekend storm could impact the Colorado Rockies' home opener at Coors Field on Friday, with the main effects likely arriving Friday night into Saturday. Forecasters say a rain-snow mix could reach the Denver metro, though specifics remain uncertain. Easter Sunday currently looks sunny and mild.

stories.headline: Denver's queer bookstore Petals & Pages closing on Santa Fe Drive
stories.summary:
Petals & Pages, a queer-focused bookstore on Santa Fe Drive in Denver, is closing, according to a report from Denverite. The store had been a fixture on the city's arts corridor, which is home to numerous galleries and independent businesses.

The closure adds to a challenging environment for independent booksellers and LGBTQ-focused retail spaces in the Denver metro area. Further details on the timeline or circumstances of the closing were not immediately available from the brief report.

weather.notable: About 10 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: Something is dreadfully wrong. I seem to have come to a comedy club.

topStory.headline: Trump Threatens Iran Infrastructure as War Expands, Civilian Casualties Mount
topStory.summary:
President Donald Trump on Monday threatened to destroy Iran's energy resources and vital infrastructure, including desalination plants, if a deal to end the ongoing U.S.-Iran war is not reached soon. Separately, video shared by Trump without comment on Tuesday appeared to show a major explosion near Isfahan, one of three uranium enrichment sites previously bombed during the conflict's opening phase in June. Iran has not formally acknowledged the strike.

Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi insisted Tuesday that Tehran's military operations target only U.S. forces, addressing his remarks specifically to Saudi Arabia after Iranian strikes have hit civilian targets across Gulf Arab nations. Araghchi shared an image purportedly showing damage to an American aircraft at Prince Sultan Air Base in Saudi Arabia, urging Arab states to expel U.S. forces from the region. Two Indonesian United Nations peacekeepers serving with UNIFIL in southern Lebanon also died Tuesday during escort operations, with two others seriously wounded; the Indonesian Ministry of Defense said the exact cause remains under investigation.

The war, which escalated following a 12-day exchange between Iran and Israel in June, has continued to spread across the region despite repeated declarations of victory by Trump. Israel reported four additional soldiers killed Tuesday in its ongoing Lebanon offensive, raising its total military death toll in the conflict to 10. Isfahan, site of Tuesday's reported explosion, is believed to hold a portion of Iran's highly enriched uranium, and U.S. officials have previously suggested the possibility of seizing it with ground forces.

Analysts and critics have noted a widening gap between Trump's public declarations and conditions on the ground. Trump has repeatedly claimed victory in the conflict at campaign-style events and in White House appearances since March, statements that have drawn scrutiny as fighting continues to intensify. The war is being closely watched for its potential impact on global energy markets and regional stability, with Gulf Arab states reportedly pressing Washington to sustain its military campaign even as Iranian strikes have struck targets within their borders.
